author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
Calcinated limestone is already a developed and widely distributed product, making milled olivine somehwat more precious than this quirky and destructive idea.  Production of quick lime, as in cement, releases CO2, but old concrete begins to absorb CO2, well after it has set.  At a quick check today retail, delivered portland cement is about $1 U.S. per pound, uncrushed Olivine stone for a sauna is about twice that amount, and crushed peridot (gem form of olivine) is about $19 per lb.  Crushed concrete rubble is as cheap as you can get it -- maybe a wind powered ball mill might produce enough more CO2 absorbing material more quickly than anyone could organize a nuclear explosion in an olivine mountain.
I can't imagine that either olivine or quick lime absorbtion of CO2 is quicker than the natural carbon cycle.  Perhaps the basis of comparison should be the rate and expense of the growing of shell by animals and plankton in sea water -- probably cultured plankton would absorb CO2 in a less damaging way. Perhaps growing  a forest or two is quicker?   I'm in favor of genetically modified, fast growing peat bogs, myself.
Perhaps another cost basis: There are industrial fas plants almost everywhere in the developed world that use compression and refrigeration to refine oxygen, nitrogen, carbon dioxide, water vapor, and argon from the atmosphere.  As rare as it is in quantity, CO2 is so cheap a product that what can't be sold for industrial use is just released.  How much might it cost for such a plant to "bottle" CO2 in non-potable water at pressure (like carbonated soda or mineral water) into weighted (and recycled) plastic bottles and simply dump them into a mile or so of deep ocean?    
Another argument against might be that the era of testing occurred at or near a recent cold point in the atmosphere, and actually ended at a time when warming seems to have picked up again.  But records (and how or if those might have been manipulated) are in dispute..
The silliest thing about the idea may be that it doesn't figure on any of the costs of the explosive, either in financial terms or environmental contamination.  The hoped-for powdered olivine might not be the only product from the process, and the current owners of such a device might be reluctant (or much too enthusiastic) to just hand over a bomb or two .  
Yet another point might be how it would be decided exactly who might be trusted to properrly set off such a detonation.  I can't think of anyone who I'd like to try this out.

author: @eddymodent303
text:
Dealing with climate change through capturing CO2 is a fraud. The problem of atmospheric CO2 would be properly addressed through sequestering pure carbon derived from biomass. Biomass is easily replaced and uses atmospheric CO2 in its formation. 

Sequestering CO2 is inherently unsafe, energy intensive, expensive, and self perpetuating as it removes twice as much oxygen from the atmosphere as carbon. Pyrolysis on the other hand, can be self supporting by burning gases produced during the process.

Biomass is readily available and quantifiable and may reduce to as much as 25% pure carbon by weight without high tech equipment and without government sponsored research - - that may be why it is ignored.

author: @MrChris20912
text:
So, the plus side might be disperse enough powdered olivine throughout the atmosphere like one very large glitter bomb. With the upside of quickly distributing a CO2 absorbing material in a ubiquitous manner, across large areas of the globe. The assumptions being that wind and weather patterns will do most of the work and not be disrupted. The results would be 40-50 years of lowered global temps aiming for 2 degrees, but could be more, depending on how much olivine gets dispersed.

The downsides, radiation in the immediate area, even with mitigation. Irradiated material fallout over a wide area. Potentially not getting as much material into the air as needed in order to get the temp reduction required. NYMBY. 

We do have the data from all of the previous atomic tests - though how much governments are working to share is unknown at present, they have built some amazing simulation movels using those fata sets. 

In theory, an intriguing idea. In practice, I'm not for it. Way too much downside for what might be gained. Now, if they take the idea and refine it a LOT more to say, use nuclear powered lazers to "mine" the material in similar bulk amounts, I could get behind that. 

Another question, what happen when the olivine dust goes into the oceans in bulk quantities? Does it stop working? How does it react? And since the oceans are already absorbing exponentially larger another of thermal energy and CO2 than the 9:13  atmosphere, is there a scenario where we can stir it into the oceans instead of explosively dispersing the olivine into the air? (Unfortunately one of the downsides being an even further short term warming of the oceans, if this was done.)

author: @crawkn
text: It's not true that a concept such as this can only be experimented with on a planetary scale, there is no reason it couldn't be tested on a much smaller scale. But I agree that doing something irreversible on a planet-wide scale all at once is not a great idea. We are learning new and surprising things about geology and atmospherics on a regular basis, so we can't be confident in our assumptions.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: You can't accurately simulate all aspects of a planet on a smaller scale or via software.

replies.author: @crawkn
replies.text: @vitordelima I know that, but you can learn something about whether it would even work. Obviously if you don't even get the intended effect on a small scale, you won't scale it up. But as I said, I think its a pretty bad idea with probability of numerous unintended consequences and uncertainty about positive effects.

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: @crawknBut you can never be sure about the side effects.

replies.author: @crawkn

replies.author: @dr.froghopper6711
replies.text: Sorghum-Sudan grass produces huge amounts of biomass for several different uses. New Mexico State University has done research that shows that, if the grass is mowed down to about 10” long after having grown to 24” first, it puts down up to five times as many roots below ground. That’s CO2 sequestered in the ground with the added benefit of the biomass produced above ground. The grass can be used for animal fodder, compost, living ground cover. It’s a warm weather grass that is drought resistant.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
LoL. CO2 was higher during the Ice Ages. The problem is that CO2 is not the driving factor of global warming. If it were, we would never have had the Ice Ages, when CO2 levels were much much higher. "The Ordovician Period is notable for having high levels of CO2 while also experiencing an ice age, specifically the Andean-Saharan glaciation. This ice age occurred around 460 to 420 million years ago and is one of the major ice ages in Earth's history. The Late Ordovician glaciation is particularly interesting because it occurred during a time when CO2 levels were relatively high, estimated at around 4,200 ppm. The occurrence of an ice age during a period of high CO2 concentrations is somewhat counterintuitive given the current understanding of the greenhouse effect."
   Current levels of Co2 are 10x lower than during that Ice-Age. So, it is quite obvious that CO2 cannot be the main driving force of global warming... we need to look elsewhere.
Of course, I am totally opposed to using nuclear energy to explode dust everywhere... the least of which is that it will make the dust radioactive for centuries.
I don't know of a single nuclear test site that currently enjoys a thriving farming community.
